entities.name: Board for Advance Rulings (Income Tax)
entities.statutory_basis: Income Tax Act 1961, Chapter XIX-B (ss.245N–245V) as substituted by Finance Act 2021 s.98 — reconstituted as 'Board for Advance Rulings' from 'Authority for Advance Rulings'. Two Boards constituted with retired ITAT Presidents/Vice Presidents. Original AAR (single-member, retired SC Judge as Chairman) abolished by Finance Act 2021 w.e.f. 01.04.2021.
entities.data_quality_notes: Independence concern: Finance Act 2021 reconstitution replaced retired SC Judge Chairman with senior IRS officers — structural shift from judicial to departmental composition. Contested by taxpayer bar (pending writ petitions in several HCs challenging constitutionality of non-judicial composition for advance rulings). Two Boards (Board-I at New Delhi, Board-II at New Delhi) constituted by notification. Rulings binding on applicant and CBDT (s.245S) but subject to writ jurisdiction of HC under Art.226/227. No appeal to ITAT or HC as of right. created_year reflects original AAR (Finance Act 1993 Chapter XIX-B); reconstitution year 2021.

unverified_fields.note: CBDT notification 92/2021 PDF URL not verified against CBDT notifications archive.

sources.label: Finance Act 2021 — s.98 substituting Chapter XIX-B of IT Act 1961
Income Tax Act 1961 — Chapter XIX-B (ss.245N–245V as amended)
CBDT — Board for Advance Rulings constitution notification 2021

appointment.tenure: Board Chairman: retired officer of Indian Revenue Service (IRS) at Commissioner level or above. Members: IRS officers. Tenure and terms as prescribed under s.245-OB rules. Contrast pre-2021 AAR where Chairman was retired SC Judge.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: As per IT Act s.245-OB and rules; members are IRS officers subject to service rules and departmental proceedings. No independent removal committee.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: Board members are public servants (IRS officers) and subject to Lokpal jurisdiction unlike judicial members of pre-2021 AAR.
complaint_mechanism.source: IT Act 1961 s.245-OB; Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act 2013 s.14

Generic entity representing the Assessing Officer cadre across all Income Tax field formations. AO is the originating authority in the IT appellate chain: AO → CIT(A) → ITAT → HC → SC.

entities.name: Board of Revenue (Generic)
entities.statutory_basis: Constituted under state-specific Board of Revenue Acts or Revenue Codes. Principal examples: Board of Revenue Act (Uttar Pradesh) 1922 (as amended); Rajasthan Board of Revenue Act 1949; Madhya Pradesh Land Revenue Code 1954 (Part II); Tamil Nadu Board of Revenue Abolition Act 1980 (abolished in TN — see structural_variations). Exercises highest revisional/appellate jurisdiction over revenue/land records, land acquisition compensation, and tenancy matters within the state.
entities.data_quality_notes: Generic node. Boards of Revenue are quasi-judicial state bodies sitting atop the revenue courts hierarchy (Tehsildar → SDO → Collector → Commissioner → Board of Revenue). Not all states retain a Board of Revenue; Tamil Nadu abolished it (1980), Karnataka merged functions into Revenue Appellate Tribunal, and some NE states never had one. Appeals from BoR orders lie to the respective HC. created_year 1951 is approximate (many BoRs predate Independence; reconstituted under post-1950 state statutes). ministry_responsible left null — state Revenue or Land Records Department is the administrative parent; no universal entity_id mapped in graph yet.

sources.label: UP Board of Revenue — official website

complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: Members are state government officers; state Lokayukta jurisdiction depends on state-specific Lokayukta Act definition of 'public functionary'. Mark contested pending state-specific review.

entities.name: Customs Authority for Advance Rulings
entities.statutory_basis: Customs Act 1962 ss.28EA–28EI as inserted by Finance Act 2021 s.94 — established two CAARs (Mumbai and New Delhi). Replaced the pre-2021 Authority for Advance Rulings (Customs) (which shared infrastructure with the income-tax AAR and was headed by a retired HC/SC Judge). Finance Act 2021 reconstituted as officer-level body within CBIC.
entities.data_quality_notes: Independence concern: same as aar_income_tax — Finance Act 2021 reconstitution shifted from retired HC/SC Judge to serving IRS/CBIC officer. Ruling is binding on applicant and CBIC for 3 years (s.28EH) but subject to HC writ. No statutory appeal. Two CAARs constituted at Mumbai and Delhi per CBIC Notification 77/2021-Cus (13.08.2021). Number of rulings issued and pendency figures not publicly available in structured form.

sources.label: Finance Act 2021 — s.94 inserting ss.28EA–28EI in Customs Act 1962
CBIC Notification 77/2021-Cus — CAAR constitution at Mumbai and Delhi

appointment.tenure: Principal Commissioner of Customs or Commissioner of Customs (IRS-C&CE cadre) designated as Authority. Terms of service per IRS service rules; not fixed-term independent appointment.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Officers subject to IRS cadre service rules; no independent removal procedure. Transfers and postings through DoPT/CBIC.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: CAAR members are serving IRS officers; public servants under Lokpal Act.

entities.name: Central Vigilance Commission
entities.statutory_basis: Central Vigilance Commission Act 2003, s.3
entities.data_quality_notes: CVC originally established 11 February 1964 by executive resolution on the recommendation of the Santhanam Committee on Prevention of Corruption. Given statutory footing by CVC Act 2003 (Act 45 of 2003). created_year is 1964 (original establishment); statutory_basis reflects the operative 2003 Act. CVC Act 2003 s.4 appointment committee consults minister_home_affairs (Union Minister of Home Affairs), not ministry_home_affairs — same distinction as PHRA 1993 s.4 for NHRC. ministry_responsible is null — CVC is an independent statutory body reporting to Parliament (annual report laid before each House under CVC Act 2003 s.25); it is not under the administrative control of any ministry. case_volume not populated. No AppealableTo edge — CVC is a supervisory/investigative body with no appellate hierarchy above it; judicial review lies via Article 226/32. Former duplicate entity id 'cvc' retired; merged into this record.

sources.label: Central Vigilance Commission Act 2003 — s.4 (appointment committee)

appointment.tenure: 4 years from date of assumption of office, or age 65, whichever earlier; CVC Act 2003 s.6

complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: CVC is itself an anti-corruption oversight body; CVC Commissioner is not a public servant within Lokpal Act 2013 s.14 in the executive sense. Flag as Contested if a contrary position is formally argued.

jurisdiction_types: Appeals against CCI orders under Competition Act 2002

entities.statutory_basis: Competition Act 2002 §§ 53A–53U (as inserted by Competition (Amendment) Act 2007, in force 20 May 2009). Abolished by Finance Act 2017 (§ 172 — Tribunal, Appellate Tribunal and other Authorities (Qualification, Experience and Other Conditions of Service of Members) Rules 2017 read with Finance Act 2017 § 160 NCLAT amendment). Jurisdiction transferred to National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) by Finance Act 2017 with effect from 26 May 2017.
entities.data_quality_notes: COMPAT was operational from 15 May 2009 (date Chairperson assumed charge) to 26 May 2017 (date NCLAT took over CCI appeal jurisdiction). Appellate chain (historical): CCI order → COMPAT → Supreme Court (§ 53T). Post-abolition chain: CCI order → NCLAT → Supreme Court. The Finance Act 2017 (§ 172, Sixth Schedule) amended Competition Act 2002 § 53A to substitute 'National Company Law Appellate Tribunal' for 'Competition Appellate Tribunal'. All pending matters transferred to NCLAT. Historical note: The abolition of COMPAT was part of a broader rationalisation of appellate tribunals recommended by the Law Commission (272nd Report, 2017) and carried out via the Finance Act 2017 omnibus amendments.

entities.name: Court Martial (Generic — Army/Navy/Air Force)
entities.statutory_basis: Army Act 1950 (Chapters VI–IX); Navy Act 1957 (Chapter VIII); Air Force Act 1950 (Chapter VI); Armed Forces Tribunal Act 2007 (established AFT as external appellate body post-2007).
entities.data_quality_notes: Generic stub covering GCM, SGCM, DCM, SCM convened under all three service Acts. Key structural gap: Summary Court Martial (SCM) has no right of appeal to AFT — the accused's sole remedy is post-conviction petition to Chief of Army Staff / Central Government under Army Act 1950 § 164. This is a significant accountability lacuna. Pre-AFT (before 2009 operationalisation), no external judicial body existed; High Courts exercised writ jurisdiction only. Appellate path (post-2007): finding/sentence → Confirming Authority → AFT (for GCM/DCM/SGCM). SCM sentences not appealable to AFT (AFT Act 2007 § 15 read with Army Act § 164). Appointment of court martial members: Convening Officer (generally a senior officer) convenes and nominates members; Judge Advocate (a JAG-branch officer) is appointed to advise on law. No civilian element in composition. No public docket or cause list; proceedings not published. judge_strength not modelled — court martial is an ad-hoc body, not a permanent court with sanctioned judicial posts.

appointment.tenure: Ad-hoc per case

bias_complaint_to.mechanism: Statutory appeal / revision to AFT under Armed Forces Tribunal Act 2007

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Convening authority may dissolve court martial before finding; Judge Advocate General review of proceedings.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: Military judicial officers are not public servants within Lokpal & Lokayuktas Act 2013 for purposes of court martial jurisdiction.
complaint_mechanism.source: Army Act 1950 §§ 108–153; AFT Act 2007

entities.name: Dispute Resolution Panel (Income Tax)
entities.statutory_basis: Income Tax Act 1961, s.144C as inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2009 s.42. DRP is a collegium of three Principal Commissioners / Commissioners of Income Tax designated by CBDT. Not a separate statutory tribunal — constituted as an alternative dispute resolution mechanism within CBDT for eligible assesses (primarily foreign companies and transfer-pricing cases).
entities.data_quality_notes: Independence circularity concern: DRP members are CBDT officers adjudicating objections to draft assessment orders passed by CBDT officers. Directions issued by DRP under s.144C(5) are binding on the Assessing Officer but do not preclude ITAT appeal (s.144C(10) — assessee may appeal to ITAT against final order; department may not). DRP is NOT a tribunal distinct from CBDT — it is an internal collegium. Classifying as RegulatoryBodyQJ in regulatory_bodies cluster to reflect quasi-judicial function within the executive; type CentralTribunal would mischaracterise independence. DRP panels constituted at major cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Pune, Ahmedabad) per CBDT notifications. No public annual report. Pendency data not publicly disaggregated from ITAT inflows.

sources.label: Income Tax Act 1961 — s.144C (inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2009)

appointment.tenure: Members designated by CBDT from serving Principal Commissioners / Commissioners of Income Tax. No fixed tenure; rotational designation at CBDT discretion.

entities.name: Employees' Provident Fund Appellate Tribunal
entities.statutory_basis: Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 1952, s.7D (as amended). EPFAT hears appeals against orders passed by EPF authorities under ss.7A, 7B, 14B of the Act. Seat at New Delhi. Note: Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 1952 and EPFAT are continued under Social Security Code 2020 (enacted but not yet fully notified/operative as of June 2026; 1952 Act remains governing law).
entities.data_quality_notes: EPFAT is a single-bench tribunal at New Delhi adjudicating appeals from EPFO order under EPF Act ss.7A (determination of dues), 7B (review), 14B (damages). Appeals from EPFAT lie to HC under Art. 226/227 and to Supreme Court under Art.136. Social Security Code 2020 (enacted; rules under consultation) will eventually subsume EPFAT into broader Social Security Appellate Tribunal framework — transition not yet implemented. Pendency data not publicly available in structured form. Website: https://epfat.gov.in/.

entities.name: Employees' Provident Fund Organisation
aliases: Employees' Provident Fund Organisation of India
entities.statutory_basis: Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 1952, ss.5A–5AA (Central Board of Trustees), ss.5D–5E (Central Provident Fund Commissioner). EPFO exercises quasi-judicial functions through its officers under ss.7A (determination of EPF dues — Enquiry Officers), 7B (review), 14B (imposition of damages). These officer-level adjudicative orders are appealable to EPFAT. EPFO itself is a statutory executive body; only its adjudicatory officers have quasi-judicial character.
entities.data_quality_notes: EPFO is classified ExecutiveBody (not CentralTribunal) because it is primarily an executive/statutory fund management body. Its quasi-judicial enforcement function (ss.7A, 14B orders) is exercised by designated officers whose orders feed EPFAT's appellate jurisdiction. EPFO administers EPF, EPS (Employees' Pension Scheme 1995), and EDLI (Employees' Deposit Linked Insurance Scheme 1976). Manages ~280 million subscriber accounts (as of 2024 EPFO annual report); collects ~INR 1.8 lakh crore annually. Social Security Code 2020 will reconstitute governance structure when notified.

entities.name: Employees' State Insurance Court (Generic)
entities.statutory_basis: Employees' State Insurance Act 1948, ss.74–82 (Chapter VII — Adjudication of Disputes and Claims). ESI Courts are designated by the respective State Government from existing civil or judicial magistrate courts; no separate infrastructure. Jurisdiction over disputes as to ESI contributions, benefits, penalties. Appeals from ESI Court lie to the respective High Court (s.82).
entities.data_quality_notes: Generic node for approximately 150 ESI Courts designated under ESI Act s.74 across industrialised states. Not a separately constituted tribunal — existing civil courts designated by state governments. Exact count and list of designated courts not centrally published by ESIC. Appeals from ESI Court → respective HC under s.82. cluster: subordinate_courts is appropriate (designated civil court jurisdiction); type: SpecialCourt reflects the specialised ESI jurisdiction designation. funding via State_Consolidated_Fund (court infrastructure) while ESIC (central body) provides administrative support — mixed funding pattern not separately quantifiable. ministry_responsible left null (each state's Law/Labour Dept. designates; no single central entity_id applicable).

appointment.tenure: Presiding Officer: a Judge of a Civil Court of the rank of a Munsiff / Civil Judge (or higher) designated by the State Government under s.74(3). Not a separately created post — designation of existing judicial officer.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Designated judicial officer; complaints through High Court administrative supervision channel. No special removal mechanism under ESI Act.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: Designated judicial officer exercises judicial functions; excluded from Lokpal jurisdiction in judicial capacity.
complaint_mechanism.source: ESI Act 1948 ss.74–82

structural_variations.description: Each state with ESI coverage has one or more ESI Courts, typically co-located with existing civil courts at district HQ or industrial town. Number varies by state industrial activity (~150 designated courts nationally estimated).

structural_variations.independence_risk_note: Designated civil/magistrate courts with own docket; ESI matters added as additional jurisdiction. Capacity strain where industrial disputes heavy (e.g. Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, Karnataka).

entities.statutory_basis: Registered Society under Societies Registration Act 1860. Established by Ministry of Law and Justice; functions under Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996. ICADR Rules 1996 (as amended) govern proceedings.
entities.data_quality_notes: ICADR was established in 1995 as an inter-governmental body on recommendation of the UN General Assembly (UNGA Res 40/72, 1985) for Asia-Pacific region. It is distinct from IIAC (formerly NDIAC), which is a statutory body under the New Delhi International Arbitration Centre Act 2019 (renamed by Act 23 of 2022). Appellate path: arbitral award → challenge petition to High Court (§ 34) → appeal to Division Bench of HC (§ 37) → Supreme Court (SLP). entities.statutory_basis: Press Council Act 1978 (§ 4 — establishment; § 14 — quasi-judicial powers)
entities.data_quality_notes: Press Council Act 1978 repealed and replaced the Press Council Act 1965 which had established the first PCI in 1966; re-established under 1978 Act. Quasi-judicial function: PCI can censure a newspaper/news agency/editor or journalist but has no punitive power beyond censure — it cannot impose fines or criminal penalties (§ 14). Appellate path: PCI censure order → challenge by writ petition to High Court. Structural gap: PCI's jurisdiction covers only print media; digital/online news portals and TV news channels are outside its statutory remit as of 2026 (pending Press and Registration of Periodicals Act 2023 implementation, which modernises registration but does not expand quasi-judicial scope to digital media). The PRP Act 2023 (in force) supersedes PRB Act 1867 for registration but does not dissolve or restructure PCI.

entities.name: State Election Commission (Generic)

jurisdiction_types: Superintendence, direction and control of elections to Panchayats and Municipalities
Election dispute quasi-judicial functions (where state law provides)

entities.constitutional_basis: Article 243K (Panchayats) and Article 243ZA (Municipalities) — inserted by Constitution (73rd Amendment) Act 1992 and Constitution (74th Amendment) Act 1992, both in force from 24 April 1993.
entities.statutory_basis: State Election Commission Act / Rules enacted by each state legislature; varies by state. Generic pattern: State Commissioner appointed by Governor, removal procedure mirrors Election Commission of India (requires SC reference per Art. 243K(2)).
entities.data_quality_notes:
This is a generic stub covering the common constitutional pattern for all 28 States and 3 UTs with legislatures (J&K, Puducherry, Delhi). Key variations across states:
  — Some states have merged SEC with general election administration (not best practice).
  — Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh SECs have faced litigation over non-constitution of
    panchayat bodies for extended periods.
  — J&K SEC reconstituted after J&K Reorganisation Act 2019.
Appellate path for election disputes: The bar on civil court jurisdiction (Arts. 243O, 243ZG) means election disputes go to state-constituted Election Tribunals → High Court (second appeal / writ) → Supreme Court (SLP). State-specific SEC entities (e.g. sec_maharashtra) should be modelled as separate entities with state_data fields when state packs are created. judge_strength not applicable (SEC is a single-member body by constitutional design).

appointment.tenure: Prescribed by state law (typically 5 years or age 65)

bias_complaint_to.mechanism: Election disputes under panchayat/municipal elections: election petition before Election Tribunal constituted by state, not civil court (per Art. 243O / 243ZG). Bias or misconduct complaint against SEC: writ petition to High Court or petition to SC under Art. 32.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: State Election Commissioner may only be removed by Governor on grounds of misbehaviour after SC inquiry per Art. 243K(2) — identical protection to High Court judges. Standard is therefore very high.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: State Election Commissioners have constitutional removal protection analogous to HC judges; Lokpal Act 2013 Schedule exclusions and general jurisprudence suggest lokpal jurisdiction does not extend to constitutional offices of this nature. Contested; maintainer should verify state-wise.
complaint_mechanism.source: Constitution of India Arts. 243K, 243ZA; state SEC Acts

entities.name: State Industrial Tribunal (Generic)
entities.statutory_basis: Industrial Disputes Act 1947, s.7A (Industrial Tribunals). State Governments constitute State Industrial Tribunals to adjudicate matters in the Second and Third Schedules of the Act (Third Schedule includes wages, hours, bonus, profit- sharing, retrenchment, closure). State Industrial Tribunals have wider jurisdiction than Labour Courts. Distinction from CGIT (s.7A): State-sphere industries are referred to State Industrial Tribunals; Central-sphere industries to CGIT. Industrial Relations Code 2020 (enacted; not yet operative as of June 2026).
entities.data_quality_notes: Generic node for State Industrial Tribunals (state sphere) constituted under IDA 1947 s.7A. Distinction from cgit_* nodes: these handle State Government references for state-sphere industries (industries not in the Central sphere list per IDA s.2(a) read with Ministry of Labour orders). Appeals from State IT awards lie to respective HC under Art. 226/227; no statutory appeal route. Awards enforceable as Civil Court decrees (s.17A). Industrial Relations Code 2020 will replace this structure when notified. Count: 20–30 benches nationally; exact figure not centrally published. ministry_responsible left null — each state's Labour Department.

appointment.tenure: Presiding Officer: person who is or has been a Judge of an HC (IDA 1947 s.7A(3)). Tenure as prescribed by State Government.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: State Government may remove on grounds of misbehaviour or incapacity after inquiry (IDA 1947 s.9).
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: State Lokayukta jurisdiction varies by state-specific Lokayukta Act. Mark contested pending state-specific review. Presiding Officer exercises judicial function; many state acts exclude such roles.
complaint_mechanism.source: IDA 1947 ss.7A, 9

entities.name: State Labour Court (Generic)
entities.statutory_basis: Industrial Disputes Act 1947, s.7 (Labour Courts). State Governments constitute Labour Courts to adjudicate matters in the Second Schedule of the Act (including propriety or legality of orders under Standing Orders, discharge or dismissal of workmen, withdrawal of customary concessions, illegality/irregularity of strikes or lockouts). Industrial Relations Code 2020 (enacted; rules under finalisation as of June 2026) will replace s.7 when notified — 1947 Act operative.
entities.data_quality_notes: Generic node for State Labour Courts constituted under IDA 1947 s.7. Total estimated at 150–200 benches nationally across industrialised states; exact count not centrally published by MoLE. Appeals from State Labour Court awards lie to the respective HC under Art. 226/227 (writ) — no statutory appeal. Awards are also enforceable as decrees of a Civil Court (IDA s.17A). Industrial Relations Code 2020 will reconstitute Labour Courts when notified. ministry_responsible left null — each state's Labour Department; no uniform central entity_id.

entities.statutory_basis: Trade Marks Act 1999 §§ 6–9 (Registry and Registrar); Trade Marks Rules 2017.
entities.data_quality_notes: created_year: 1940 — Trade Marks Registry established under Trade Marks Act 1940 (original establishment). Current operative statute is Trade Marks Act 1999 (§§ 6–9); use 1999 only if modelling the current Act regime rather than registry lineage. statutory_basis field reflects the 1999 Act. Trade Marks Registry head office: Mumbai. Branch offices: Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, Ahmedabad. The Registry is subordinate to CGPDTM (patent_controller entity in JEM). Quasi-judicial function: Registrar hears trade mark opposition (§ 21) and rectification proceedings (§ 57); these are appealable to HC post-IPAB abolition. Pre-2021 appellate path: Registrar → IPAB → HC → SC. Post-2021 appellate path: Registrar → High Court (§ 91, as amended by Tribunal Reforms Act 2021) → Division Bench → SC. Relationship to patent_controller: trade_marks_registry is a sub-office under CGPDTM; suggest AdministrativeSupervision edge trade_marks_registry → patent_controller for maintainer wiring.

entities.constitutional_basis: Constitution of India, Chapter VI — High Courts in the States
entities.data_quality_notes: Permanent bench at Lucknow; parent hc_allahabad. Populate judge_strength complaint_mechanism populated from SC In-House Procedure 1999 and SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto). criminal_prosecution.requires_sanction_from set to governor_state per Veeraswami (1991) for HC judges. lokpal_jurisdiction No per operative Feb 2025 stay; revisit if SC decides finally. audit.conduct_oversight_body set to in_house_procedure_sc (same procedure governs HC judges). and case_volume from HC website / DoJ / NJDG.

appointment.tenure: Until age 62 (Article 217)

bias_complaint_to.mechanism: In-House Procedure (SC Resolution 1999, extended to HC judges). Complaint lodged with the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court; if the allegation is against that Chief Justice, complaint filed directly with the CJI. CJI may constitute a 3-judge in-house committee to inquire. Report is confidential. Complainant has no locus in inquiry proceedings.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Address by Parliament — Article 217(1)(b) read with Article 124(4). Motion by 100+ LS MPs or 50+ RS MPs. No HC judge has been successfully removed in Indian history.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: SC stayed Lokpal's attempt to assert jurisdiction over HC judges (suo moto, Feb 2025). Lokpal had separately ruled in Jan 2025 that it cannot hear complaints against SC judges (Art 124 basis); the Feb 2025 stay extends the same bar to HC judges. Status: active litigation, but operative bar currently in place.
complaint_mechanism.source: K. Veeraswami v Union of India (1991) 3 SCC 655; SC In-House Procedure 1999; SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto re HC judges)

NJDG snapshot case_volume merged complaint_mechanism populated from SC In-House Procedure 1999 and SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto). criminal_prosecution.requires_sanction_from set to governor_state per Veeraswami (1991) for HC judges. lokpal_jurisdiction No per operative Feb 2025 stay; revisit if SC decides finally. audit.conduct_oversight_body set to in_house_procedure_sc (same procedure governs HC judges).

appointment.tenure: Until age 62 (Article 217)

bias_complaint_to.mechanism: In-House Procedure (SC Resolution 1999, extended to HC judges). Complaint lodged with the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court; if the allegation is against that Chief Justice, complaint filed directly with the CJI. CJI may constitute a 3-judge in-house committee to inquire. Report is confidential. Complainant has no locus in inquiry proceedings.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Address by Parliament — Article 217(1)(b) read with Article 124(4). Motion by 100+ LS MPs or 50+ RS MPs. No HC judge has been successfully removed in Indian history.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: SC stayed Lokpal's attempt to assert jurisdiction over HC judges (suo moto, Feb 2025). Lokpal had separately ruled in Jan 2025 that it cannot hear complaints against SC judges (Art 124 basis); the Feb 2025 stay extends the same bar to HC judges. Status: active litigation, but operative bar currently in place.
complaint_mechanism.source: K. Veeraswami v Union of India (1991) 3 SCC 655; SC In-House Procedure 1999; SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto re HC judges)

NJDG snapshot case_volume merged complaint_mechanism populated from SC In-House Procedure 1999 and SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto). criminal_prosecution.requires_sanction_from set to governor_state per Veeraswami (1991) for HC judges. lokpal_jurisdiction No per operative Feb 2025 stay; revisit if SC decides finally. audit.conduct_oversight_body set to in_house_procedure_sc (same procedure governs HC judges).

appointment.tenure: Until age 62 (Article 217)

bias_complaint_to.mechanism: In-House Procedure (SC Resolution 1999, extended to HC judges). Complaint lodged with the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court; if the allegation is against that Chief Justice, complaint filed directly with the CJI. CJI may constitute a 3-judge in-house committee to inquire. Report is confidential. Complainant has no locus in inquiry proceedings.

complaint_mechanism.removal_mechanism: Address by Parliament — Article 217(1)(b) read with Article 124(4). Motion by 100+ LS MPs or 50+ RS MPs. No HC judge has been successfully removed in Indian history.
complaint_mechanism.lokpal_jurisdiction_note: SC stayed Lokpal's attempt to assert jurisdiction over HC judges (suo moto, Feb 2025). Lokpal had separately ruled in Jan 2025 that it cannot hear complaints against SC judges (Art 124 basis); the Feb 2025 stay extends the same bar to HC judges. Status: active litigation, but operative bar currently in place.
complaint_mechanism.source: K. Veeraswami v Union of India (1991) 3 SCC 655; SC In-House Procedure 1999; SC Stay Order Feb 2025 (suo moto re HC judges)

entities.name: e-Committee of the Supreme Court of India
entities.statutory_basis: Constituted by Government of India in pursuance of proposal from Chief Justice of India. No separate statutory basis — functions under the administrative authority of the SC and DoJ MMP framework.

appointment.tenure: At pleasure of CJI — no fixed tenure

complaint_mechanism.source: Not applicable — e-Committee is policy/oversight body, not adjudicatory

digital_infrastructure.integration_notes: e-Committee sets standards; NIC (under MeitY) builds and maintains. High Courts are Implementing Agencies under their respective jurisdictions. The Central Project Coordinator (CPC) in each HC is accountable to the e-Committee via the PPMS portal (ecourts.nic.in).

structural_variations.description: The e-Committee is chaired by a sitting SC judge (CJI-designated), making it a judicial body. However, eCourts Mission Mode Project is monitored and funded by Department of Justice, Ministry of Law & Justice (executive). This creates a dual-principal problem: e-Committee sets technology policy; DoJ controls the budget. Every technology decision of scale requires DoJ sanction. No formal dispute resolution mechanism exists between the two bodies for technology disagreements.
structural_variations.source: ecommitteesci.gov.in; doj.gov.in/division/ecourts/
structural_variations.independence_risk_note: The executive (DoJ/MeitY) controls the budget and the technical vendor (NIC). The SC (via e-Committee) controls the policy. This structural tension means the judiciary's technology backbone is ultimately dependent on executive goodwill and budget allocation cycles. No SLA or contractual obligation exists between NIC and the courts.

structural_variations.description: Several HCs have developed custom Case Management Systems that do not fully conform to NIC CIS standards: Bombay HC (custom CMS), Delhi HC (enhanced digital services), Madras HC (independent IT development). Data from these HCs enters NJDG in non-standard formats, reducing national data reliability. This is a documented standardisation failure.
structural_variations.source: eCourts Phase III documentation; doj.gov.in
structural_variations.independence_risk_note: HC autonomy (Article 235 administrative independence) conflicts with the e-Committee's mandate for uniform national standards. No enforcement mechanism exists against an HC that builds its own system.

DoPT (ministry_personnel_dopt) administers the search panel; CVC may provide integrity inputs on candidates but is not a statutory member of the selection committee post-2014. Vineet Narain (1997) directions informed earlier procedure. "Caged parrot" characterisation is well-documented. State consent withdrawal list should be verified against latest state notifications.

Prakash Singh v Union of India — police reform directives

structural_variations.description: Under DSPE Act Section 6, CBI cannot exercise jurisdiction in a state without that state's general or specific consent. As of 2025, several states have withdrawn general consent (Maharashtra, West Bengal, Chhattisgarh, Rajasthan, Jharkhand, Mizoram, Kerala, Telangana). This means CBI can only investigate in those states when directed by the SC or the relevant High Court.
structural_variations.source: DSPE Act 1946, Section 6; respective State Government notifications
structural_variations.independence_risk_note: State consent withdrawal structurally restricts central investigation in states with different political dispensations. Creates a gap in central accountability where state police investigates instead.

relationships.notes: The Collegium system is entirely judge-made — not written in the Constitution. Article 124(2) says President appoints "in consultation with" CJI and other judges. SC in Second Judges Case (1993) held this consultation is binding; CJI's recommendation is determinative. This reversed SP Gupta (1981) which had held executive supremacy. The 99th Amendment NJAC (2014) attempted to change this but was struck down in Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record v Union of India (2015).

sources.label: Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record v Union of India (1993) 4 SCC 441

relationships.notes: Formal instrument of appointment issued by President. In practice, President acts on Collegium's recommendation. Post-1993, President cannot appoint a judge the Collegium has not recommended.

NJAC was constituted by 99th Constitutional Amendment. Would have replaced the Collegium with a 6-member commission including CJI, 2 senior SC judges, Law Minister, and 2 eminent persons. Struck down by SC 5-judge bench in Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record v Union of India (2015) as violating the basic structure of judicial independence. This relationship is HISTORICAL — rendered as faded/ghosted in the UI when time slider is set after 2015.

sources.label: Supreme Court Advocates-on-Record v Union of India (2015) 5 SCC 1

relationships.notes: For HC judge appointment, the recommending Collegium involves: CJI + 2 most senior SC judges + HC Chief Justice + most senior HC judge elevated to SC. Governor and State Government consulted but their consultation is advisory only — not binding on the Collegium.

Governor (acting on advice of State Council of Ministers under Art. 163) is consulted on HC judge appointments. This consultation is NOT binding. This is the primary formal channel for state government input into HC appointments — but its non-binding nature means a state government cannot veto an appointment the Collegium has recommended.

Governor appoints, posts and promotes District Judges in consultation with the High Court. HC's consultation is binding (K. Ashok Reddy 1994). For direct recruitment from bar: Governor acts; HC dominates. For promotions from lower service: Governor acts on HC recommendation.
